events.event_summary: Fox is acquiring Roku for $22 billion in a major bet to scale its streaming/CTV footprint.
primary_drivers: M&A re-rating of CTV/streaming platform peers
Consolidation reduces independent ad-supported streaming options, shifting ad budgets and competitive dynamics
Removal of a key neutral distribution platform changes leverage for content owners and smart-TV rivals

beneficiaries.rationale: Roku going under a single content owner (Fox) creates conflicts for advertisers wanting neutral CTV measurement/buying; The Trade Desk's independent DSP and OpenPath become more strategically valuable for CTV ad spend.
Magnite is Roku's SSP partner and a pure-play CTV SSP — deal validates CTV platform value and Magnite re-rates as the next scarce independent CTV ad-tech asset; possible takeout target itself.
Connected-TV pure-play with sports tilt — re-rates on CTV M&A multiple read-through (Fox paid up for streaming distribution); also a potential consolidation candidate.
A Fox-owned Roku could deprioritize Netflix tiles/recommendations on the #1 US streaming OS, modestly pressuring sub-acquisition efficiency on that surface.

losers.rationale: Disney loses a neutral, scaled distribution partner — Hulu/Disney+ now distributed on a platform owned by direct rival Fox, weakening negotiating leverage on carriage economics and ad inventory splits.
Max distribution on Roku now runs through a competitor; WBD's already weak streaming scale position worsens versus a combined Fox+Roku ad-supported powerhouse.
Counter-risk: Roku's first-party ad inventory and ACR data may get pulled in-house to Fox's ad stack, reducing programmatic supply available through independent DSPs.
Comcast/NBCU's Peacock loses leverage on the largest US connected-TV OS now controlled by a content rival; also raises antitrust attention on its own potential streaming M&A path.

headlines: Fox Bets $22 Billion On Roku In Streaming Power Grab
